result:
I’d like to talk about the quiet magic of **small moments** — those brief, unassuming instants that slip by unnoticed most of the time, yet somehow stitch together the tapestry of a life.  

It’s the way morning light falls on a coffee cup, turning the surface into a moving mirror of gold.  
It’s the unexpected scent of rain on hot pavement, or the sound of a distant train whistle cutting through a silent night.  
It’s the pause before a shared laugh, the glance between friends when a song they love comes on, the gentle weight of a cat curling into sleep on your lap.  

Modern life trains us to look for the milestones — the promotions, the trips, the big celebrations. We document them, measure them, try to hold onto them. And yet, when I look back, what often glows brightest in memory are not the loudest moments, but the quiet ones: sitting on the porch with my grandmother shelling peas, watching snow fall outside a window with nowhere to go, hearing my father hum an old tune while fixing something in the garage.

There’s a kind of gentle rebellion in noticing these small things — in choosing to be present in a world that’s always pushing us toward the next thing.  

It reminds me that meaning doesn’t always announce itself with fanfare. Sometimes it whispers. Sometimes it waits in the space between tasks, in the breath before speaking, in the soft, ordinary grace of a Tuesday afternoon.  

Maybe paying attention to these fleeting, fragile bits of time is a way of loving the world. A way of saying: *I see you. I’m here. This matters.*

So here’s to the small moments — the unsung, the unphotographed, the deeply human.  
May we notice them.  
May we live in them.  
And may we carry their quiet light with us,  
long after they’re gone.
